publisher: Smart Growth America / National Complete Streets Coalition
measurement_note: Report uses fatalities per 100,000 people (per capita) rather than the US government's standard of deaths per vehicle-mile-traveled (VMT), arguing VMT lets rates look 'better' simply because people drive more.

demographics.note: National pedestrian fatality rates per 100,000 people by group, 2020-2024. Not broken out by metro/state.
demographics.income_note: Report states fatality rates are highest in lowest-household-income areas but does not give a single numeric multiplier like it does for race.
demographics.race_disparity_note: American Indian/Alaska Native rate is 3.7x the national average; Black Americans are killed at 1.7x the national average. Black and Native populations are 16% of US population but 22% of pedestrian deaths, 2020-2024.

international_comparison.peer_benchmark_note: Rate the US would reach if it had matched the average reduction rate of 34 comparable peer nations, 2015-2024.

Norway.note: Lowest roadway fatality rate in the world, 2015-2024

Canada.note: Less than half the US rate

case_study.location: Jackson Avenue and Hollywood Street, Memphis, TN
case_study.context: Real intersection with multiple pedestrian fatalities, used by the report as a before/after illustration

methodology.trend_field_meaning: Change in per-100k fatality rate from 2015-19 average to 2020-24 average; positive = got more dangerous
